Genesis, neuron, neurogenesis. And the classic textbooks would say there is no neurogenesis in the mammalian brain, at least in the brain itself.
There might be in the peripheral nervous system, but not in the central nervous system.
You know, it's kind of a puzzle, right? Because if you look across different species, you do see quite clearly that there's neurogenesis in many other animals, in insects, in fish, and in birds.
And I love the bird literature, because they've really talked about this for a long time.
It turns out that in songbirds, there are certain parts of the brain that have been studied exquisitely for learning and memory.

As I said, that's a really prominent topic in the neurosciences, and there's certain clusters of neurons in the bird brain that have been identified that are absolutely critical for the birds to learn their own song, okay?

When people look in these bird brains, they see that actually, this is really critical to their survival, and yet from year to year, 50% of those neurons will die off, and 50% of the neurons you see in any given year are brand new neurons.
They've undergone neurogenesis, okay? So here we have mammals, no neurogenesis is the claim.
Here we have birds, 50% of the neurons, of course they are, our evolutionary divergence is very far back, but the question is, why would birds have such huge turnover?
Any hypotheses, anyone wanna offer a hypothesis about that? Okay, ah, I had one other thing to say to you.

Okay, so why? So one idea is that, you know, birds do a lot of migration.

Told you there's lots of brain cells up there, and maybe, you know, getting rid of 50% of the neurons in the birdsong regions is actually a way to reduce their weight, and that would make them be able to migrate a lot further.

That idea, as it says here, really probably doesn't make a lot of sense, because you're only gonna be saving, you know, half of, 50% of one or 2% of your total weight, so it's probably not the driving force.

Could be, though, that, again, brain function, neural function, is really metabolically demanding for neurons to be generating action potentials, and so forth, consumes lots of our oxygen, and so, again, if you get rid of the neurons, you're gonna reduce the metabolic demands.
That was one idea, but what really seems to sort of be the answer is that birds, right, when they, with their songs, to prove their fitness, they actually reacquire songs from one year to the next.

It might be a way that they can sharpen their song, or they can demonstrate their flexibility, but it really seems to be about the relearning, the reacquisition of the song is one way that seems to be a selective pressure for how, say, female birds are judging the fitness of the male birds, and so forth, and there's quite a bit of evidence to suggest that, indeed, it really seems to be something about song production and song learning that promotes all this neurogenesis in the birds.

Of course, they're both correlational analyses, so we've got to be cautious about inferring causation, but just showing that the number of new neurons that you generate, where's my cursor here?

some neurogenesis going on in the mammalian brain. And there certainly were some lone wolf scientists who in the 1970s and 80s were also writing that there was neurogenesis in the mammalian brain, but it just couldn't catch on until these new tools came along.

But in addition, they've come up with this new retrovirus, GFP+, whatever that means.

This is only going to be evident in newly formed cells.

Well, what they do here is they did this first visualization two days after the injection, but then they come back and actually visualize other animals 30 days after the injection, and now look what happens.
This green dye, the red dye is only filling up the cell bodies.
This particular green dye actually goes through the whole cell of the new cells, and now you see it's a beautiful neuron.
So this is really convincing that you not only have neurogenesis, or you not only have new cells developing in the hippocampus, but that these new cells are actually neurons.

Well, since then, it's actually translated that that was, of course, rats, but now there's actually really impressive evidence that there's some neurogenesis, some significant amount of neurogenesis going on in the human brain, and again, this came about from the tools of molecular biology, and in this case, it was completely serendipitous, and it's because, again, what they had done is they were developing another tracer that identified newly forming cells, the so-called BRDU, so again, it's similar to the other one.

It specifically labels new cells, not new neurons. We don't know that, but any new cell, and this was actually developed for part of cancer treatment or cancer monitoring because, of course, cancerous cells are rapidly developing, proliferating, and so they wanted to have a tracer that could allow people to visualize the spread of tumors, but when they actually then looked in the brain, they actually saw that look.

Cancer might be someplace else in the body, but in some of these patients who had died, they looked in their brains and they saw there was this expression of the BRDU in the brain cells of these individuals.
Again, this is a post-mortem, unexpected analysis, and what's shown here, again, is the green is the BRDU.
There's not a lot of it here. Red is labeling all the neurons.
We have that same question about, you know, is the green labeling the neurons?
Lots of work been done that's really kind of made clear that these are neurons, so that sort of changed the story that there is neurogenesis, and the questions have now shifted.

One question is certainly, why does neurogenesis seem to be more prominent in some parts of the brain compared to other parts of the brain, and at present, you know, the places where you have the most prominent neurogenesis, at least as the current debate, there's some folks who say it's everywhere, but it seems to be strongest in the hippocampus and the olfactory bulb.
So the olfactory bulb is actually part of our olfactory, our smelling system.
There's different reasons for why people think you might want to have a lot of neurogenesis there.
It's about it being a chemical sensor in that you may want to have, like, sort of fresher cells in the olfactory system.

The hippocampus is perhaps the... the most interesting story here that seems to be emerging, because the hippocampus is really critical for acquiring new information, something we'll be talking about in the memory section.
It's not maybe as essential for the stabilization or the recall of long-term memories, but really important for the formation of new memories.

And so, you know, you could spin a story and say that I really want to have a lot of new cells in the hippocampus that keeps the system fresh to continually being acquired new information across the lifetime.

Now, again, like I say, the story's really changed. Now that there's, I think, wide acceptance that neurogenesis exists, lots of work asking about, you know, what are the factors that influence the likelihood of neurogenesis?
You can see that there's going to be translational value here.
We know we have a lot of neurological disorders like Parkinson's disease or Alzheimer's disease or ALS where we have degeneration of the brain, of certain cell types in the brain.
So if we could come up with ways to promote neurogenesis, these could be obviously excellent treatments for people of, you know, even in a stroke if you have a loss of brain tissue.
So first basic question is, you know, is neurogenesis something that's just happening, just naturally, randomly evolving?
Or can we actually promote neurogenesis? So lots of work done like this, you know, basically here they're taking rats.

Another group of rats that they call in an enriched environment, they give them a bunch of wheels, a bunch of little ladders to go up and down, things that can be stimulating for the rats.
And they're basically showing here that if you're the control animal, that's in black here.
Again, this is all the counts of cells with BRDU.
But what they also do then is they inject both the BRDU label and the neuron label.
And so all the gray ones are cells that are neurons that are kept with BRDU, that are co-labeled by the two different tracing agents.
So we know that all of these are neurons. These probably include many other cell types.
And the main story here, right, is that you see that in the enriched environment, you really seem to be promoting more neurogenesis.

main effect? What about enrichment? Well, again, the gray bars here are the unenriched, so I take the average of those two, it's somewhere around here.

Being in an enriched environment also promotes neurogenesis. But the question of an interaction is, do we have a different effect of one variable on the other as a function of the value on the other variable?

And again, stem cells are those cells that are sort of in the earliest stage of development.
They can basically develop into any cell type in the body, depending on the context that you place them in.
And so the notion is, again, if you place stem cells into the brain, are you going to actually promote neurogenesis?
Okay? And here what they've done is, this is quite common, when they have a dopamine depleted rat.
Basically, depleting dopamine is making like a model rat system of Parkinsonism.
Because that's the, pathology in Parkinson's, one of the things is you lose your dopamine cells and it makes it very hard.
People with Parkinson's disease can't start moving. They have great difficulty initiating a movement.
And so again, these dopamine depleted rats are like Parkinsonian rats.
They move very slowly and so forth. And so what they're going to ask here is, they're going to actually do an injection of either stem cells or saline.
That's their control condition. They're trying to do everything the exact same, but one case the little thing they inject that contains stem cells, the other one is just salt water.
And then they're going to ask the behavioral benefit. And again, what you see on the Y axis here is the time for the animal to initiate a movement.
So lower value means you're faster. You can initiate faster.
It's better to have a small initiation time. Okay. You can see at the, right before they start the experiment, these rats are really slow.
It's taking them 10 seconds to start whatever this behavior is.
What you see now is with the stem cells, okay, at week four, at week six, and at week eight, you actually get a major improvement.
And presumably it's not the stem cells that's actually leading to this initiation, but they have other measures to show that these stem cells are turning into dopamine cells.
So they're getting some recovery from that dopamine depletion from those injections.

You really got to look out at eight. And that's why people say you really always want to do active controls.
You want to do something like do the exact same surgery on your control animals or the same injection so that you'll be able to see things like this kind of, it's not really a placebo effect.

I think it really is just the act of having mucked around in the brain and stuck a needle in and so forth and injected the substance has actually promoted the residual tissue to function better, at least for a few weeks.
